title: Share of population living in extreme poverty: September 2024 data vs. latest data
subtitle: Extreme poverty is defined as living below the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line), which is $2.15 per day in 2017 prices and $3 per day in 2021 prices. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).
caption:
Share of population living in extreme poverty: September 2024 data vs. latest data

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line, which is $2.15 per day in 2017 prices and $3 per day in 2021 prices. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This line chart compares two global estimates of the share of the population living in extreme poverty from 1990 to 2026. The vertical axis measures the percentage of the population, ranging from 0% to 50%, while the horizontal axis marks the years. Two distinct lines represent different data series: a blue line labeled "Latest data (2021 prices)" and an orange line labeled "Sep 2024 data (2017 prices)." The blue line begins in 1990 at a value slightly above 40% and follows a downward trajectory, crossing the 20% mark around 2010 and ending near 10% by 2026. The orange line starts lower, just under 40% in 1990, and remains consistently below the blue line throughout the entire period. It drops below 30% around 1996, falls beneath 20% near 2006, and concludes around 9% in the final years shown. Both lines exhibit a general decline over time, with minor fluctuations visible in the mid-1990s and around 2020. The data relates to income or consumption per capita depending on the country and year. The visual gap between the two lines persists across the timeline, with the 2017 price series consistently showing lower percentages than the 2021 price series.
